author: @SolwiseMD
text:
Though Robert will be unlikely to read this post I make it because it may be of interest to other readers. A few points about the series.

Magnetic Circuit.
If you look at a commercial motor (or generator) you will see that great effort goes into reducing the gap between the moving and stationary parts of the magnetic flux path down to about half a milli-metre or so.  I suspect this requirement is much more difficult (read: expensive) to achieve on the outside edge of a wind turbine than on a self-contained generator connected to the shaft.
Having said that, Robert's rig does decelerate under load (which is what we want) so there is some hope.
In order to improve the performance it would be possible to reduce the gaps in the magnetic circuits:  Firstly by getting the transformer core as close as possible to the magnets, and secondly by arranging an iron 'keep' (perhaps the removed side of the transformer) on the other side of the magnets to minimise the gap on that side.  I can't see how to arrange this on the current layout but a better result could be achieved by rotating the magnets so that they face outwards, hanging below the lower rim of the rotor so that the 'keeps' could be mounted on the base, inward and opposite the re-oriented transformers.

Low winds and Starting
If we do all of the above and achieve good coupling of the magnetic circuit we will find that the 3kg pull from each magnet will cause the rotor to click around in steps.  In a worst case this could prevent the rotor starting in low winds.  It may be possible to alleviate this effect by making the position of the coils fine-adjustable so that phase of the 'clicks' from each coil is cancelled by all the others.  The more coils the better.  This effect is usually addressed in commercial solutions by using a single coil on the rotor instead of permanent magnets.  This arrangement is called an alternator rather than a generator.  It needs some sort of regulator to control the rotor current and hence the output voltage.  The regulator also prevents any current flowing until the rotor is rotating at an adequate speed.

replies.author: @q.e.d.9112
replies.text:
Betz law does not specify the type of turbine. It’s the limit that a perfect, 100% efficient, rotor could achieve. You simply cannot catch all the kinetic energy in wind because to do so you would have to stop it dead. But that would block any further wind from passing through your rotor. It turns out that 59% of the total energy in the wind is the most you could possibly get. Try to slow the wind any further to extract more power and you affect the upwind speed so that it has less energy to give.
So a 100% efficient rotor could extract about 59% of the total kinetic energy of the wind. The most efficient HAWT systems can manage about 80% Betz efficiency, thus capturing only about 48% of the wind’s total kinetic energy. Savonius rotors such as Robert’s get at best ~25% Betz efficiency.

author: @q.e.d.9112
text:
Hi Robert!
Sorry to keep correcting you, but you’ve got your constant wrong by a factor of ~2.
Assuming ρ = 1.19 kg/m^3 as the density of air, the constant 16/27 x 1/2 x 1.19 = 0.35, not 0.18 as shown. What value did you use for ρ ?
Now to calculate Betz power limit for a 1 m^2 turbine, [height x diam for VAWT,  (π x diam^2)/4 for HAWT]

Airflow (m/s).        Betz limit (W)
        1                               0.35
        2                               2.8
        3                               9.45
        4                              22.4
        5                              43.75
        6                             75.6
        7                            120.5
        8                            179.2
        9                            255.15
       10                           350

       15                        1,181 

        20                       2,800

        25.                      5,469 

I think your turbine has a different area, but if you express that area in square metres then multiply the above figures by your area you’ll get Betz power limits for your turbine.

Being absolutely honest with you, you’ll be doing well if you output more than a fifth of those figures.If your turbine operates at 20% efficiency it will be pretty good for a Savonius type design. 75% efficiency in your generator, would be amazing for a home made generator designed around unmatched components. Which brings you to somewhere around 15% of the Betz limit. Higher efficiency requires Aerodynamic lift as in  the Darrieus design. 

Do not be disappointed if you get perhaps 35-40W max in a 10 m/s breeze. I’m sure you’ve hoped for more, but the big boys operate at 80% Betz efficiency and perhaps 95% electrical efficiency, and even then they’re taking less than 50% of the wind energy that passes through their rotors.

By all means keep trying. Savonius rotors can be durable and rugged, which they need to be if you’re going to get useful power out of them. Suitable for high wind environments such as mountains and Antarctica. Not that useful, elsewhere. Possibly good, in conjunction with solar panels, high eff. LEDs and Li-ion for a remote street light, weather stations, comms towers etc.

Finally, add my voice to those others on the power/energy question. Power (W, Watt) is the instantaneous production/consumption of energy (J, Joule). A 60W light bulb uses 60J of energy _every_ second. If I multiply the power by the time, as in my 60W bulb switched on for 20 seconds>> 60W x 20s = 1200J. 
Now a Joule is a pretty small unit. You eat a third of a million of them with your morning muesli. So the electrical industry came up with the kW.h. It’s the amount of _energy_ created/consumed when there’s a kW of _power_ for an hour. 1000W x 3600s = 3,600,000J or 3.6MJ.
A genuine industry standard 1kW wind turbine is able to put out that power at a wind speed of 10m/s (~22mph). Even at the Betz limit you would need a turbine area of 2.86m^2 to achieve that performance. In practice, considerably larger.
Please do not take these remarks of mine in anything other than the helpful spirit they are intended.
Kia Ora
Micko
Aotearoa-NZ

replies.author: @q.e.d.9112
replies.text:
@MrSummitville 
The Betz limit is independent of the type of rotor. It’s the limit of extractable power from a given airspeed across a given area, regardless of the method used. It reflects the fact that to extract all the KE you would have to reduce the wind speed to zero which would block incoming air. This limit is approximately 59% of the total kinetic energy in one second’s worth of wind passing through that area. If we try to slow the wind down by more than about 25% of its initial velocity we start to hinder the passage of the air and get diminishing returns. 
It’s how you can compare the efficiency of different systems. A wind turbine capable of capturing 59% of the total energy would be 100% efficient. Of course, one of the reasons drag turbines, such as the Savonius type, have such low efficiency is, as you pointed out, that they’re only using one half of the available wind. In fact the other half is actively reducing energy extraction. However, the stresses on the structure increase relative to that total area, and this is the area we need to consider when comparing different rotors.
Savonius rotors have some advantages. They can be built to operate over a greater range of wind speeds, and they are largely unaffected by wind shear. They are quiet, present little danger to birds and can be built both simply and robustly. Their maintenance requirements are generally far less than lift turbines such as HAWTs or Darrieus type VAWTs, but their output is far lower. Good for remote, low energy requirements, such as unmanned weather stations in areas where solar panels are unsuitable. Great for high latitude winters. Fine for low power requirements. But, using the Betz limit as 100% efficiency, the VERY best HAWTs can attain ~80%, the best Darrieus types ~60% and the very best Savonius around 25%. This is shaft power. There are inevitable, further losses in converting that shaft power to electrical power.All of these efficiencies only apply at the designed wind speed. At higher or lower wind speeds the efficiency will be lower, though a well designed, variable pitch HAWT may be able to operate close to maximum across a range of wind speeds.
There are good reasons why HAWTs have a higher efficiency than VAWTs and equally good reasons why Darrieus are more efficient than Savonius. That’s not to say improvements cannot be made, but Savonius is ALWAYS going to come third in the efficiency stakes because drag effect rotors are inherently less efficient than lift effect ones.

ETA
Yes, my Watts calculations were based on an area of one square meter, and the 20% efficiency was just about the upper limit for Savonius designs.

replies.author: @q.e.d.9112
replies.text:
@MrSummitville 
They may claim an efficiency of 67% “in the real world”, but the real world is governed by some pretty solid physics. Betz law is about wind NOT devices. Extracting kinetic energy from wind, however you do it, requires the air leaving your “windprint” to be slower than when it arrived. But this slowing of the air leaving your device creates a logjam that reduces the speed, and hence the energy, of the air arriving at your device. The upshot is that when you rob the wind of 59% of its original energy, ANY attempt to take more will result in a situation where you may reduce the outlet velocity further (that’s the ONLY way you could possibly extract more energy) but, in doing so, you lose more energy in your “choked” upstream flow than you gain by extracting more from your exhaust stream.
I stress again, this is nothing to do with your device.
In reality I’ll bet that “REAL WORLD” 67% is actually 40% = 67% of 59%.

As to VAWTs being able to better HAWTs efficiency, there are good reasons why this is unlikely to be the case. The first is that the upwind side of the VAWT has already taken a proportion of the energy from the wind before it reaches the downwind side. Secondly the angle of incidence remains constant on a HAWT and may be variable to provide efficiency across a range of wind speeds, but it’s constantly varying and reduces to zero, twice for each blade in every revolution on a VAWT. Thirdly, the forces acting on the blades of a HAWT are relatively constant, but those on a VAWT shift 180° every half revolution. This can cause vibration, excessive wear and possibly fatigue failure.
Don’t get me wrong. I like VAWTs because even a sophisticated Darrieus is simpler to engineer than a HAWT.
I think they look better, particularly the ones with S shaped blades and of course they are better than HAWTs in disturbed air flows such as in urban environments.
But that said, I suspect a £100 solar cell would produce a hell of a lot more electricity over the course of a year than any £100 wind rig. 
Overall, I think Robert is having fun and so are most of the people watching this. I think, as has been suggested by another commenter, that it would have been beneficial if he had done some more research. But it’s all part of a learning curve. I suspect he could be in for another when he starts to measure the output from his little generator.

author: @cookiecrumble2245
text: The only way I see that the Betz limit can be beaten is by taking advantage of ground effect. In other words taking the exhaust off the blades after they have done the work to drive another rectangular set of blades. In HAWT it can be achieved with - two sets of 2 blades rotating together - the front leading edge of the first set of 2 blades are moved from the conventional position-  so the front blades 2 leading edges are along the diameter, centre line. Then cleverly fix another set of blades very slightly behind the first both blades at the same angle - going in the same direction - this time with the trailing edge along the same diameter centre line. The first set of 2 blades exhaust onto the second set of 2 blades as they rotate. The second set of 2 blades acting as ground effect for the first set of 2 blades - reducing drag. If adopted would transform efficiency of wind energy production.

author: @robertcringely7348
text: Betz is a crock. Large commercial turbine manufacturers use Betz to game the market. They use it to claim that the most efficient turbine has three blades. Have you noticed how 30 years ago the world settled on three-blade industrial turbines? That's because Betz "proved" that three blades were ideal. This pushed utility turbines to larger and larger diameters until now they are so big that only very large enterprises can afford to make them. Put the three biggest blades you can transport down the road to the tallest tower you can build and you are there, they say. Except the really important measure isn't specific power in kilowatts, it is power produced in kilowatt-hours-per-year. The supposed ideal Betz turbine, then, has three huge blades, is rated at the speed as you say, but it only produces power (net power, because the alternator consumes power to energize the electromagnets) above a starting speed and below a maximum speed above which the turbine is stopped to avoid damage at the very point where it is making the most power. More blades are better. More turbines are better. Wind fields are designed with the towers 10 times their height apart to avoid them failing and smashing each other. Is that rule-of-thumb the best way to produce power? No. Is it really even based on facts? No. Think of a field of turbines, not a single turbine. Make more smaller turbines with, say, 10 blades each. Use permanent magnet generators, not alternators. Eliminate all computer controls (the greatest single cost of all in any wind field is computer control) and let them start generating from zero mph on up to whatever the wind gives them. Ten blades reduce the starting resistance so your turbine starts easier, and generates power 24/7/365 creating far more power than Betz (or GE or Siemens) could even imagine.

Wind trapping funnels, rotary wind deflectors and variable pitch blades can all help increase the efficiency because wind, left to its own, is going to go as much AROUND the unit as THROUGH it, AND [just by nature] favor neither side, AND the pitch of the blades being uniform, as we all know, creates drag to the one side of the rotor which can be somewhat mitigated by switching  the pitch of each rotor via a cam [or, for those who love electronic gadgetry and have no problem vastly increasing the complexity of their turbines] some kind of digitally or analog controlled pitch system [which would probably rob more power than it gained, although I can not say that for a fact].

1: The wind trap will force a greater percent of the wind to actually pass through the  rotor, instead of passing around it, which will cause the wind to exert more force upon the blades,

2: A diverter, which can either be used in conjunction with a trap, or without, will cause the wind to rotate in the direction of the rotor, increasing the force to the side  you need it on, while decreasing it away from the side you need it to not blow on, and these can be made either as a fixed, omnidirectional shroud around the rotor, or as a bias flow, trailing edge tiller steered diverter, which, like the wind trap [and would/could normally be used in CONJUNCTION WITH the wind trap, and would typically be steered <again> only by the wind, and therefore exert no parasitic losses (unless, again, a person chose to use some kind of powered actuator in place of just letting the wind do its own job)]

3: A blade deflection cam, which would usually be aimed using the same method of a steering tiller vane trailing the rotor acts in conjunction with some small linkages, affixed to the vanes, which, depending on where they are in relationship to the direction of the tiller [and thus to the wind] works so as to increase the opposing pitch angle on the leading edge, which by default, increases the working surface area the wind is acting against [see the formula Robert posted there] while REDUCING the angle of attack on the opposite side of the rotor, which, therefore, reduces the LOSSES experienced on this side of the equation, which, in our overall function would  be counted a 'drag' and work against the aims of rotating the whole assembly.

By combining all three of these adaptations [and it can  easily be done] one can greatly increase the power and efficiency of the final product.

       /)/) 
    (  'ˬ') 
@(>_ )-b 
     \) \)

replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Rob, I started in Wind Power about 15 years ago. I started with Savonius turbines because I just thought they were cool. Still do. replies.author: @q.e.d.9112
replies.text:
Dave W 
Betz law applies to both, equally. It’s the maximum amount power that can be extracted by a 100% efficient turbine of a given area. For a horizontal axis that is the area of the propellor disc, π.r^2, where r is the length of the blades. For a VAWT, the area is the diameter of the rotor by the height (d,h) in the equation at 2:10.
😉

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text:
I enjoyed this video and look forward to the next.  Perhaps where there is disagreement is also a misunderstanding of KW vs KWh.  I think your critics are, perhaps without realizing it, coming from the knowledge that winds are rarely sustained at any steady speed, You are entirely correct to measure and rate your project with the same assumptions as commercial projects.  I don't think Betz' law will silence the critics who correctly observe that wind isn't constant.   Promoters of subsidized wind power generation do not explain that the 25ms wind speed occurs less than 30% over the service life of the wind turbine, and the maximum service output of a single generator is less than 0.3 times the KW rating .  Your approach of using inexpensive materials (with distributed, point-of-use generation) is much more economically feasible and sustainable.
I wonder if rating at 25ms velocity is useful, as this is more or less equal to 90 kph or 55 mph wind speed.

replies.author: @newtonbomb
replies.text: I think he means an average of 1kWh a day when deployed under average windspeed conditions.  If you can build a turbine that can produce an average of 100W for 10 hours of projected wind loading then you have a 1kWh of energy per windy day.  Because wind conditions are so variable, wind turbines are rated in kWh for this reason, during a 24hr period it may be producing 180W sometimes, 40W another, 80W another, and sometimes 0W when there is no wind; but produces a total of 1 kWh over the whole day of generation.

replies.author: @saiello2061
replies.text:
@Tom Dixon You only need raw Kilowatts generated at particular wind speeds to nail a wind turbines performance. KW is a power rating, adding a time factor like KWh(hours) or KWm(months) turns it into  an energy rating measured in Joules generated for that time period. For wind turbines, how much energy that can be generated over a given time period depends on average wind speeds for your specific location and to a lesser degree, how much can be stored depends on the efficiency of your battery system. Having an average energy generated rating, separate from the raw power ratings, for a particular location makes sense because then you can determine if a wind turbine is worth the investment over time. 

To be honest, I don't understand why energy usage is given in an intermediary unit such as KWh for example, as you might find on your gas or electric bill, surely it's much easier to just give it in Joules and save having to do any calculations... Maybe someone can tell us the reason for this...? 😁
